_title: The Gap Between Peace and Numbness
_raw_content: Most people mistake numbness for healing. The nervous system shuts down and calls it calm. But real peace has texture — it breathes, it can feel everything and still remain. Numbness is the body's refusal to feel. Peace is the body's capacity to hold it all. The gap between them is where most people stop and call themselves healed.
slide_1_hook: You didn't find peace. You went numb.

slide_2.text:
The nervous system has a trick.

When feeling becomes too much, it doesn't resolve.

It shuts down.

And shutdown feels quiet.

slide_3.text:
You stop reacting.
You stop crying.
You stop feeling the old wound.

And you call that growth.

slide_4.text:
Peace and numbness live in the same silence.

One is alive.
One is frozen.

Most people never learn the difference.

slide_5.text:
Real peace has texture.
It breathes.
It can feel everything — and still remain.

Numbness can't feel anything at all.

slide_6.text:
Healing isn't the absence of feeling.

It's the nervous system's capacity to hold all of it — without collapsing, without shutting down.

If you feel nothing, you haven't arrived.
You've disappeared.

caption:
There's a moment in every healing journey where the pain stops. And everyone around you says you look better. You feel steady. Controlled. Still.

But something is off.

You're not at peace. You're in shutdown.

The nervous system learned that feeling was dangerous — so it turned the volume to zero. Not because you healed. Because you couldn't afford to feel anymore.

Numbness is the body's oldest protection. And it mimics freedom almost perfectly.

Almost.

Because real peace doesn't feel like nothing. It feels like everything — held. Regulation isn't the absence of sensation. It's the capacity to stay present inside of it.

If your healing made you feel less — not lighter, but less — that's not the finish line.

That's the next door.
